summary: Editor: Toggle full screen not working since 4.5.dev5

description.text:
### Tested versions

Broken since 4.5.dev5 and still present in 4.5.beta6.

### System information

Android

### Issue description

On the Godot Editor for Android, the option Editor > Toggle Full Screen (F11 equivalent) does nothing.
Before 4.5.dev5 it worked fine, but since that version (and all subsequent betas up to 4.5.beta6), fullscreen mode is broken.


### Steps to reproduce

1. Install Godot Editor for Android (4.5.dev5 or newer).
2. Open any project.
3. Go to Editor > Toggle Full Screen.
4. Nothing happens — editor stays in normal mode.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

Not needed (can be reproduced with a fresh empty project).

summary: exported float is rounded down

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6], v4.4.stable.custom_build [4c311cbee]

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie) 13 on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce MX150 (nvidia) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8265U CPU @ 1.60GHz (8 threads)

### Issue description

When setting an exported float variable to a small value via the editor it gets rounded down to 0.0. This happens even when the Default Float Step would allow for such small increments to be entered.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Set the Default Float Step (Editor / Editor Settings... / Interface / Inspector / Default Float Step) to 0.000001 (that is 1×10^-6).
2. Attach a script to a Node.
3. Enter the following line: ```@export var f: float```
4. Set the value of ```f``` to 0.000001 through the editor.
5. Observe how when hovering over it, it shows as 0.00000099999306. (What format is this? IEEE 754 32-bit and 64-bit formats store 0.0000009999999974752427078783512115478515625 and 0.000000999999999999999954748111825886258685613938723690807819366455078125 respectively)
6. Run the scene. The value of ```f``` can be checked via the following lines:
```print_debug(f)``` and ```print_debug(f == 0.0)```
They return ```0.0``` and ```true``` respectively.
This is unexpected. I would expect ```0.000001``` or ```0.00000099999306``` and ```false``` respectively.

Additional steps to verify that it only happens to values set via the inspector:
1. Enter the following line ```var g: float = 0.000001```.
2. Check the value of ```g``` via the following lines:
```print_debug(g)``` and ```print_debug(g == 0.0)```
They return "0.00001" and "false" respectively.

This shows that the rounding happens to values set through the inspector.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[Bug.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21923689/Bug.zip)

Note that when editing the file Node.tscn, the line f = 0.000001 is nowhere to be seen.

summary: AnimationPlayer unable to animate MeshInstance3D material properties

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6]
- Works correctly in Godot 3.6.1 (and earlier)


### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Laptop GPU (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.5597) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-14900HX (32 threads)

### Issue description

When inserting keyframes into MeshInstance3D > Surface Material Override > StandardMaterial3D properties (in this case, UV1.offset, but also problem with other properties), Godot gives this error and creates a keyframe in the animation player with a red cross in it (error):
```
Type: Nil
value: <null> (Invalid, expected type: Vector3)
Easing: 1.0
```
The value can then be set correctly afterwards in the "Inspector", but setting another keyframe later in the timeline gives the same error and after setting the value manually, it does not interpolate between the values. It jumps when the keyframe is hit rather than interpolating smoothly.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open up the attached minimal project
2. Select "AnimationPlayer" node
3. Select "new_animation" track
4. Delete the active ".:surface_material_override/0:uv1_offset" animation track (trash can button)
5. Put timeline on zero
6. Select MeshInstance3D in scene tree
7. Select "Surface Material Override" in inspector, then "Edit"
8. Navigate to UV1 > Offset
9. Press the key icon to add a keyframe. Error appears and 'x' appears in AnimationPlayer
10. Click on the 'x' and set value to '0.1' in inspector
11. Move timeline to 1 second
12. Set UV1 > Offset to 1.0
13. Again, press the key icon to add a keyframe. Error appears and 'x' appears in AnimationPlayer
14. Click on the 'x' and set value to '1.0' in inspector
15. Press "<" in the inspector to go back to material properties and make sure UV1 values are visible to you
16. Press 'play' on the AnimationPlayer. Note, the values are not interpolated. There is no gentle transition from one keyframe to the next. 
17. There is a 'rotation' track to show that it is able to interpolate the rotation of the MeshInstance3D correctly### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[animationtest1.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21925435/animationtest1.zip)

summary: Add workflow support for OpenHarmony export templates

description.text:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/contributing/development/code_style_guidelines.html
-->

Follow-up to #108553.

Provides a workflow for building OpenHarmony export templates, making it easier for users to test without having to compile editors and export templates.

## Export the project

### Preparation

1. Download the editor and export templates from the [artifacts](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109834/checks/) in Actions.[^1]
2. Download Commandline Tools, which currently requires API level 17+, that is, version 5.0.5+.[^2]
    - https://repo.huaweicloud.com/openharmony/ohpm/ (No registration required)
    - https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/en/download/command-line-tools-for-hmos (Registration required)
3. After extracting all the files, you will get a directory called `command-line-tools`, whose path is the path you need to fill in `export/openharmony/openharmony_tool_path` in the editor settings.
4. Download JDK, only JDK 17 is supported.[^3]
5. Please fill in the JDK path in `export/openharmony/java_sdk_path` in the editor settings.
6. Enable `textures/vram_compression/import_etc2_astc` in the project settings;

### Export the unsigned hap/app

Adds an OpenHarmony preset in the [export menu](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/tutorials/export/exporting_projects.html#export-menu). 

Check **Advanced Options**, and then specify `custom_template/debug` and `custom_template/release` as the export templates you just downloaded.[^4]

Make sure the export path does not contain special characters such as spaces.

### Prepare the material files required for signature[^5]

Signature material files can be generated by [DevEco Studio](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/en/download/deveco-studio), but unfortunately DevEco Studio is not available on Linux.

You can also generate these files manually by following the steps below.

1. Create a keystore (.p12) file; [^6]
    ```shell
    keytool -genkeypair -alias "key_alias" -keyalg EC -sigalg "SHA256withECDSA" -keystore "store_file.p12" -storetype pkcs12 -storepass "StorePassword" -dname "CN=myapp.example.com, OU=IT Department, O=My Company, L=City, ST=State, C=Country" -keypass "StorePassword" -validity "3650"
    ```
2. Generate a certificate request (.csr) file;[^6][^7]
    ```shell
    keytool -certreq -alias "key_alias" -keystore "store_file.p12" -storetype pkcs12 -file "test.csr" -storepass "StorePassword"
    ```
3. Use the "store_file.p12" file and "test.csr" file generated in the previous two steps to apply for the certificate (.cer) and profile (.p7b) files in [AppGallery Connect](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/cn/service/josp/agc/index.html). (Registration required)

### Export the signed hap/app

Enable `build/sign`. For the meaning and filling requirements of the fields, refer to [Manually Configuring the Signature Information](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/en/doc/harmonyos-guides/ide-signing#section112371245115818).

To manually sign the unsigned HAP file exported previously, refer to [Signing a HAP](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/en/doc/harmonyos-guides/ide-command-line-building-app#section103321051433).

### Notice

The English version of the Harmony/OpenHarmony documentation may be outdated. Please refer to the Chinese version.

If you don't have a suitable test device, you can try the [cloud debugging service](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/en/agconnect/) provided by AppGallery Connect. See [云调试](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/cn/doc/app/agc-help-clouddebug-0000002235870046). But it requires you to upload signed release builds.[^8]

[^1]: Usually you need to remove a layer of compression. The debug and release templates are named `openharmony_debug_arm64-v8a.zip` and `openharmony_release_arm64-v8a.zip` respectively.
[^2]: It is recommended to download a higher version, which will give you more options when exporting.
[^3]: Reference Chinese documentation, [配置JDK](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/cn/doc/harmonyos-guides/ide-command-line-building-app#section195447475220). 
[^4]: You can also create directories in the [data](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/classes/class_editorpaths.html#class-editorpaths-method-get-data-dir) directory according to the path `export_templates/4.5.beta/` and move the export templates into the directory `4.5.beta`.
[^5]: Can be skipped if `build/sign` is not enabled in the export preset.
[^6]: Replace the string enclosed in double quotes on the command line as needed.
[^7]: Ensure the values ​​of the same parameters in the two commands are the same.
[^8]: Requirements see [前提条件](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/cn/doc/app/agc-help-clouddebug-debugapp-0000002289629821#section112564349471).

summary: [3.x] Godot 3.6.1 does not recognize JAVA_HOME

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in Godot 3.6.1 stable official [b1ba98fce]
- I don't know if this bug is present in Godot 4

### System information

Windows 11 24H2 x64 26100.4946

### Issue description

The thing is, even though the Java SDK Path is specified in the editor settings (General/Export/Android/Java SDK Path), when exporting for Android, I get the error `‘apksigner’ returned with error #1`. Since there can be several reasons for this error when signing an APK (for example, an incorrect version of Android SDK Build-Tools), I decided to run Godot with `--verbose`. And that helped me. I saw that the problem was that I hadn't set up JAVA_HOME. Of course, I knew about this, because I don't like using the global system environment variable JAVA_HOME. It clutters up my system. Even when compiling a custom template, I only set JAVA_HOME for the duration of the console session. 

What is the point of specifying the path to the Java SDK in the editor settings if I still have to specify JAVA_HOME?

summary: Back gesture fails after dismissing the keyboard In Android Editor

description.text:
### Tested versions

v4.4.1.stable.official [github](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/49a5bc7b616bd04689a2c89e89bda41f50241464) (from Google Play Store)


### System information

android 14 (phone, tablet)

### Issue description

When the Android keyboard is invoked in Godot and then closed using the back button/gesture, the very next back gesture (intended for app navigation) fails to work. A second gesture is required. This suggests a problem in how Godot handles the sequence of back events, specifically the one immediately following the dismissal of the keyboard.


### Steps to reproduce

1. Launch the Godot application on an Android device.
2. Select any text input field (for example, the "Project Filter" search bar). The Android soft keyboard will open.
3. Use the Android back gesture or the physical/virtual back button to close the keyboard.
4. Attempt to exit Godot by performing the back gesture (or pressing the back button) again.
  - Expected Result: The application should close or navigate to the previous screen (e.g., the device's home screen or previous app).
  - Actual Result: Nothing happens. The back event is ignored.

5. Perform the back gesture one more time.
  - Result: The application now exits as expected.
  
Note: It appears that on step 4, the Godot engine fails to capture or process the back event from the Android system.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/38d95afb-ff39-4606-b594-740f19b2c6e0



### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

Any Godot project with a text input field and Android back button handling will reproduce this issue.

summary: Viewport on systems with displays with scaled resolutions has sizing issues on Linux. Both Wayland and X11.

description.text: ### Tested versions v4.5.beta5.official Tested on all 3 renderers. ### System information Godot v4.5.beta5 - Fedora Linux 42 (KDE Plasma Desktop Edition) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 3 monitors - Vulkan (Mobile) - integrated Intel(R) Iris(R) Xe Graphics (TGL GT2) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-11370H @ 3.30GHz (8 threads) - 62.52 GiB memory ### Issue description Both on **X11** and **Wayland** the viewport has sizing issues, if the system has displays with scaling set in system settings. This has been tested on a laptop with 3 displays: - Screen 01: resolution 2880x1800 , scaling 175% - Screen 02: resolution 3840x2160 , scaling 150% - Screen 03: resolution 1920x1080 , scaling 100% If I understand the results correctly some issues will still be present also if the system has just one display, as long as the resolution scale set to anything but 100%. However, this is most clear if there are more than 1 display. The issue on Wayland is slightly different from issue on X11, but they have similarities. In both cases `DisplayServer.window_get_size()` returns invalid values for everything that is not the first display (it doesn't have to be the "main" display, just the first connected/recognised). It would seem that Godot takes the scale of the first found display and uses it for all scaling moving forward, regardless of the display the window is on. On **Wayland** the general behaviour is that the window size is correct, but all the sizes of 2D elements within the viewport scale incorrectly. On **X11** the general behaviour is that the window sizes are incorrect, but the scale of 2D elements vs. the viewport is correct. **In all sreenshots the window is set to be 1280x720 and the Godot icon to 128x128.** It is a 3D scene with a sphere in the centre with 2D texture rect on a panel as the UI overlay. ### WAYLAND Main Screen. Native Resolution 2880 x 1800. Scaling 1.75. Displayed viewport: 2240x1260 (after scaling: 1280 x 720) - **correct** Displayed Godot icon: 128 x 128 (after scaling: 73 x 73) - **incorrect** `DisplayServer.window_get_size()` returns 2880 x 1800 - **correct** `get_viewport().get_visible_rect().size` returns 2240 x 1260 - **this is the displayed viewport size, but we should be getting the scaled value** Second Screen. Native Resolution 1920 x 1080. Scaling 1.0. Displayed viewport: 1280 x 720 - **correct** Displayed Godot icon: 128 x 128 - **correct** `DisplayServer.window_get_size()` returns 2880 x 1800 - **incorrect** `get_viewport().get_visible_rect().size` returns 1280 x 720 - **correct** ### X11 Main Screen. Native Resolution 2880 x 1800. Scaling 1.75. Displayed viewport: 1280x720 (after scaling: 732 x 411) - **incorrect** Displayed Godot icon: 128 x 128 (after scaling: 73 x 73) - **scales correctly to viewport, but incorrect in general** `DisplayServer.window_get_size()` returns 2880 x 1800 - **correct** `get_viewport().get_visible_rect().size` returns 1280 x 720 - **this should be the viewport size, but isn't** Second Screen. Native Resolution 1920 x 1080. Scaling 1.0. Displayed viewport: 732 x 411 - **incorrect** Displayed Godot icon: 73 x 73 - **scales correctly to viewport, but incorrect in general** `DisplayServer.window_get_size()` returns 3360 x 1890 - **incorrect** `get_viewport().get_visible_rect().size` returns 1280 x 720 - **this should be the viewport size, but isn't** ### Steps to reproduce 1. Set resolution scaling in your system settings. 2. Create a new project with a 3D scene and put any 2D element in the viewport. 3. Run the project. 4. X11: the window size will be incorrect. Wayland: the 2D element size will be incorrect ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) [resolution_bug.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21898561/resolution_bug.zip)

summary: Correctly hide BlendSpace editor error panel on load

description.text: For a BlendSpace1D or BlendSpace2D editor, there exists an error panel at the bottom. This error panel isn't correctly hidden on project load, causing a blank panel with no error to be visible on the bottom of the AnimationTree, until an error is first generated and then cleared. summary: Make BlendSpace selection tool consistent with StateMachine/BlendTree functionality

description.text:
This is another in a series of pull requests that aim to enhance the usability of AnimationTree.

For BlendSpace1D and BlendSpace2D, this commit:
- Changes the default tool to the select cursor, more in line with the default in BlendTree and StateMachine.
- Changes the select cursor tool to be a hybrid, with support for Shift+LMB dragging to move the blend position cursor, just like how StateMachine's can create transitions.
- Changes the order of the tools in the editor's toolbar to be how they are in StateMachine: LMB, RMB, Shift+LMB.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a5f12b97-2249-42c4-9743-2c4bcf6e26d5

My rationale is that adding and moving points occupies most of the user's time in the BlendSpace editor, whereas blending position is mostly expected to be changed at runtime. Also, the default tool being the blend position cursor causes constant friction when navigating through nested nodes.

summary: Fix incorrect `blend_position` values in BlendSpace editor after dragging point

description.text:
There is a bug that affects both `BlendSpace1D` and `BlendSpace2D`'s editors. When a blend point is dragged, the blend_position editor above the space is correct during the drag, but is twice the transformation once the mouse is released.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/07c1578a-dec7-4cd0-bd69-e4bfa620de05

By removing a redundant call to `_update_edited_point_pos()` in the mouse release function, this is fixed.

summary: Skip subsequent `ED_SHORTCUT` definitions

description.text:
Somewhat related to #109539
`ED_SHORTCUT` defines an editor shortcut, but it's often used multiple times for the same shortcut. Example from shader editor:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/8ebf8ae23c095d4185e79602bb18a6952e192b56/editor/shader/shader_editor_plugin.cpp#L405-L408
The shortcuts are re-defined every time the menu is opened. It does some unnecessary processing, and in some cases like #109539, multiple definitions may be inconsistent.

This PR makes `ED_SHORTCUT` return the shortcut early if it was already defined. I also applied some misc codestyle fixes in related methods.

summary: Clean up ShaderEditor shortcuts

description.text:
ShaderEditor has many shortcuts that duplicate ScriptEditor shortcuts, to the point that one of them was called "Copy Script Path". But the worst thing was that the shortcuts were re-defined every time the menu was opened (`ED_SHORTCUT` does no checks whether the shortcut exists).

This PR makes shader editor share script editor's shortcuts where possible, with its own shortcuts have definitions moved to the constructor. Also I added shortcuts for some menu items that didn't have them; they have no defaults.

summary: Some properties don't have defaults, because of `validate_property()`

description.text: ### Tested versions 4.5 and earlier ### System information W10 ### Issue description Some properties don't have defaults defined. The defaults don't appear in the documentation and the properties are not revertible in the inspector: This is because `validate_property()` changes their usage, causing them to be skipped by ClassDB. https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/8ebf8ae23c095d4185e79602bb18a6952e192b56/core/object/class_db.cpp#L2229-L2232 Unfortunately just removing the condition makes _all_ properties get defaults, even ones that are never serialized not appear in the inspector. ClassDB should be able to fetch the defaults while bypassing `validate_property()`, because it's irrelevant in this context. ### Steps to reproduce 1. Create ParticlesProcessMaterial 2. Change emission shape to e.g. ring 3. Change shape properties 4. Can't revert ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

summary: Circular/Crossing reference of PackedScenes makes referred scenes unable to load

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: 4.5.beta5

### System information

### Issue description

A node can reference another scene through the script's PackedScene class export property. If such a node is in a scene that forms a circular/cross-reference relationship with the scene it references, then the editor will refuse to open any of the referred scenes. Affected scenes can only be manually edited with a text editor to fix the problem.

I was trying to utilize export properties to rid resource paths off from my scripts(used in scene switching), then a circular reference accidentally emerged and crashed nearly all of my project. The issue is also documented in #107639. I expect that the engine could handle the reference properly, or at least pop an alert before a circular reference forms.


Attempt to run the project (scene_1 is the main scene) results in error:

Godot v4.5.beta5 - Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS 24.04 on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1060 6GB (nvidia; 535.247.01) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-4570 CPU @ 3.20GHz (4 threads) - 7.69 GiB memory

### Issue description

The `anchors_preset` property of Controls has started behaving very weirdly in 4.5. (Happens in beta5 but I have been seeing it for awhile on older betas.) Seen on controls that are not the top-level control of the scene, and have their `layout_mode` set to Anchors. Here are some of the weird behaviours:

- When you set `layout_mode` to Anchors, the `anchors_preset` will automatically be set to -1 "Custom", even thought that isn't the default (the default value is 0 Top Left).
- When you manually set it to any of the preset values, it instantly snaps back to "Custom". (Clicking the anchor preset has the desired effect of moving the control, but the preset you chose goes to Custom rather than the thing you picked.) In 4.4, if you choose, for example, "Center Top", it will stay showing "Center Top".
- Clicking the "set to default" circular arrow button has no effect, it just stays on "Custom". There's no way to clear the "set to default" arrow from this property.
- As I open and save scene files in my large project, I am noticing all the time that "`anchors_preset = -1`" is being added to my .tscn files and showing up as something I need to commit to revision control (if I revert it, it just keeps coming back).

All of these are related. I think basically Godot 4.4 would remember what preset you chose, but in 4.5 no matter what you do, it just sets up the actual anchor points and offsets correctly, and then goes "well you have customized it, so I am setting it to Custom". If this is the new intended behaviour, it would be good to change the default to -1 Custom, which would then fix most of the above awkwardness (the "set to default" arrow would disappear and the "`anchors_preset = -1`" would stop appearing in my git diffs).

Possibly related to #108442 which changed something to do with the anchor presets being set to custom.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Add a new control node (e.g. Label).
2. Set Layout Mode to Anchors.
3. Choose various values in the Anchors Preset drop-down, or click the "set to default" arrow.

[anchors-preset-test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21847400/anchors-preset-test.zip)

summary: Setting theme_font_size_override on a Label node through GDScript causes a memory leak

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible on 4.4.1stable, master [ [commit](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/42224bb750176560c515a875e50a1a25d5949ddb) ], 4.3.



### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (42224bb75) - Nobara Linux 42 (KDE Plasma Desktop Edition) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 3 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX (RADV NAVI31) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-14900KF (32 threads) - 31.16 GiB memory

### Issue description

Setting a theme_override_font_size through either .set or add_theme_font_size_override("font_size", size) results in a memory allocation for each unique set call that persists even after freeing the Label node.  It seems insane that it happens consistently, but it does appear to be the truth, and triggers at least once for every unique size. It only seems to add memory the first time a font size override is made at a specific StringName, int value, regardless of whether it is being made on the same Label.

I have not tested if this occurs for other theme properties. 

This does have pretty bad implications. I discovered this when trying to understand why some programmatically created labels in a project were adding ~70MiB to my memory usage for seemingly no reason, and tracked it down to this line.

### Steps to reproduce

Make a new scene with a Node2D, attach the following and trigger with Enter.

I could not get it to trigger the first time I ran the scene on a new project, but it works all subsequent runs.

```
extends Node2D


# Called when the node enters the scene tree for the first time.
func _ready() -> void:
	pass # Replace with function body.
	

func _input(event):
	if Input.is_action_just_pressed("ui_accept"):
		print("before label spawn ", Performance.get_monitor(Performance.MEMORY_STATIC))
		var label = Label.new()
		label.text = "Test"
		add_child(label)
		print("after label spawn ", Performance.get_monitor(Performance.MEMORY_STATIC))
		var size = 100
		for i in range(100):
			print(Performance.get_monitor(Performance.MEMORY_STATIC))
			size -= i
			label.set("theme_override_font_sizes/font_size", size)
			
		await get_tree().process_frame
		print("after resizing", Performance.get_monitor(Performance.MEMORY_STATIC))
		label.queue_free()
		await get_tree().process_frame
		print("after freeing", Performance.get_monitor(Performance.MEMORY_STATIC))
```

summary: Make explicit that string comparison is by default case sensitive

description.text:
The problem here is, if you click inside the Godot editor to go to the doco for e.g. String.begins_with(), it does not state in the method description if the search is case sensitive or not. You have to scroll up to the class description, and even then it's only inferred (as it is now) by talking about variant methods that are case insensitive. In my case, I just wanted a quick answer: is begins_with() case sensitive or not? It would be overkill to add this clarification to every string method description, so making it explicit in the class description is the best option, IMO.

"are reversed, and start from the end of the string, instead of the beginning."

This is misleading. The string itself is not 'reversed'; the meaning of the 'r' variant method is 'reverse' as in 'reverse search'. As is, the doco implies a string is first reversed and then searched from the end of the string, which would return the same as the non-r method! In my edit I did not explicitly state that the 'r' method variation stands for 'reversed' because it's implicit in the description of what the method does: start from the end of the string instead of the beginning. And there is no clarification right now in the class description for what 'n' means (I assume it means '[n]ot case sensitive'). But this could be made explicit in the edit if desired: i.e. that 'r' means 'reversed'.
